county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rhode Island county formed 1747; annexed from Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1747; contains Bristol, Warren, Barrington. RI counties have no governmental functions — towns are the primary local jurisdiction
county_metadata.research_tips: Rhode Island State Archives and Bristol town clerk. Bristol County was annexed from Massachusetts in 1747 — check Massachusetts records (Plymouth Colony, Bristol County MA) for pre-1747 families. The three towns of Bristol, Warren, and Barrington each maintain their own town clerk records for land, vital records, and probate. Bristol Historical and Preservation Society has local manuscripts and photographs. Roger Williams University in Bristol has some local history collections. The Haffenreffer Museum (Brown University, formerly in Bristol) had Native American collections relevant to Wampanoag/Pokanoket history. King Philip's War (1675-76) devastated this area — check Plymouth Colony records. FamilySearch has microfilmed Bristol town records from the 1680s. NEHGS has indexed early Bristol vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: FamilySearch microfilmed town clerk records; individual town clerk offices for originals

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rhode Island county formed 1750; formed from Providence County; contains East Greenwich, Warwick, West Warwick, Coventry, West Greenwich. RI counties have no governmental functions — towns are the primary local jurisdiction
county_metadata.research_tips: Rhode Island State Archives and Kent County town clerks (East Greenwich, Warwick, West Warwick, Coventry, West Greenwich). Kent County was formed 1750 from Providence County. Town clerks hold land evidence records (deeds), vital records, and probate records for each town. East Greenwich was a colonial seaport — check maritime and shipping records at Rhode Island Historical Society. Warwick was founded 1642 by Samuel Gorton — early town records at Warwick City Hall and RIHS. Coventry's textile mill records document the Industrial Revolution era. West Greenwich has early rural settlement records. Rhode Island Judicial Archives (Pawtucket) holds Kent County Superior Court records. FamilySearch has microfilmed Kent County town vital records. NEHGS has published early vital records for East Greenwich and Warwick.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original colony (Rhode Island and Providence Plantations)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rhode Island county formed 1703; one of two original counties created 1703; contains Newport, Middletown, Portsmouth, Tiverton, Little Compton, Jamestown. RI counties have no governmental functions — towns are the primary local jurisdiction
county_metadata.research_tips: Rhode Island State Archives and Newport town/city clerks. Newport County includes Newport, Middletown, Portsmouth, Tiverton, Little Compton, and Jamestown — each town maintains its own records. Newport Historical Society has an exceptional manuscript collection including colonial merchant records, shipping logs, and synagogue records (Touro Synagogue, 1763, oldest in US). Redwood Library and Athenaeum has colonial-era materials. Newport was a major colonial slave-trading port — check shipping manifests and merchant papers for enslaved persons research. Quaker (Friends) meeting records are extensive for Newport and Portsmouth from the 1650s. Little Compton and Tiverton were annexed from Massachusetts (Plymouth Colony) in 1747 — check MA records for earlier families. Naval War College and Naval Station Newport records for military families. Rhode Island Judicial Archives holds Newport County court records. FamilySearch has microfilmed Newport town records from the 1630s. NEHGS Early Vital Records series covers Newport County towns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: FamilySearch microfilmed town clerk records; individual town clerk offices for originals

county_metadata.parent_county: Original colony (Rhode Island and Providence Plantations)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rhode Island county formed 1703; one of two original counties created 1703; contains Providence, Cranston, Pawtucket, Woonsocket, and 12 other towns/cities. RI counties have no governmental functions — towns are the primary local jurisdiction
county_metadata.research_tips: Rhode Island State Archives, Providence City Archives, and town clerks throughout the county. Providence County is the largest and most populous county, containing Providence, Cranston, Pawtucket, Woonsocket, North Providence, East Providence, and many other towns. Providence City Archives holds city records from 1636 (Roger Williams' founding). Rhode Island Historical Society (RIHS) in Providence is the premier manuscript repository with extensive family papers, business records, and church records. Brown University's John Hay Library has significant manuscript collections including colonial-era materials. Providence Public Library's Rhode Island Collection has city directories from 1824 and local history resources. Each of the 16 towns/cities in Providence County maintains its own town clerk records. Woonsocket has strong French-Canadian heritage — check L'Union Saint-Jean-Baptiste records and Quebec parish registers. Pawtucket was the birthplace of the American Industrial Revolution (Slater Mill, 1793) — textile mill and labor records at RIHS and Slater Mill Museum. Rhode Island Judicial Archives holds Providence County court records. FamilySearch has microfilmed Providence town records from the 1630s. NEHGS has extensive indexed RI vital records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rhode Island county formed 1729; originally Kings County (1729), renamed 1781; contains South Kingstown, North Kingstown, Westerly, Narragansett, Charlestown, and 4 other towns. RI counties have no governmental functions — towns are the primary local jurisdiction
county_metadata.research_tips: Rhode Island State Archives and Washington County town clerks (South Kingstown, North Kingstown, Westerly, Narragansett, Charlestown, Hopkinton, Exeter, Richmond, New Shoreham/Block Island). Originally Kings County (est. 1729), renamed Washington County in 1781. South Kingstown was the judicial center. Narragansett Indian Tribe records are significant — the Narragansett maintained a presence in Charlestown and South Kingstown. Pettaquamscutt Historical Society (South Kingstown) has local records. Block Island (New Shoreham) has records of an isolated island community from the 1660s. Washington County was plantation country — the Narragansett Planters operated large farms with enslaved labor (unusual for New England). Check estate inventories and wills for enslaved persons at town clerk offices. University of Rhode Island (Kingston) Special Collections has regional manuscripts. Westerly has strong connections to Stonington, CT — families crossed the border frequently. Rhode Island Judicial Archives holds Washington County court records. FamilySearch has microfilmed town records. NEHGS covers South Kingstown, North Kingstown, and Westerly vital records in their published series.
